summary:
The agent identifies that endothelin-1 acts as a vasodilator when released into circulation but becomes an important mediator of vasoconstriction through receptor binding upon release—particularly under high-volume states such as those seen in chronic kidney disease or acute illness like shock. In this context, it argues that elevated levels triggered via mechanical forces within vessels contribute directly to increases in systemic vascular resistance and thus play a significant part in explaining sudden surges in arterial pressures commonly witnessed among dialyzed populations undergoing treatment cycles where fluid removal occurs rapidly over short periods without adequate compensatory mechanisms being engaged immediately post-treatment initiation phase.

The summary provided above contains redundant information not present in the original text; please revise accordingly based strictly only on what was stated originally.

Corrected Summary:

There is a role for endothelin-1 in hemodynamic changes during hemodialysis because it is produced by endothelial cells under volume overload and shear stress, leading to vasoconstriction and increased vascular resistance—which corresponds to known episodes of intradialytic hypertension. This pathophysiological link offers a biologically plausible explanation for hemodynamic instability during dialysis even though no studies have yet measured ET-1 dynamically throughout actual hemodialysis procedures.
